def generate_signals(df, params):
    import pandas as pd
    import numpy as np
    
    lookback = params['lookback'][0]
    entry_z = params['entry_z'][0]
    exit_z = params['exit_z'][0]
    
    # Compute rolling mean and standard deviation of close
    df['ma'] = df['close'].rolling(window=lookback).mean()
    df['std'] = df['close'].rolling(window=lookback).std()
    
    # Z-score of price relative to moving average
    df['z'] = (df['close'] - df['ma']) / df['std']
    
    # Entry: when price is extremely overextended (z > entry_z) -> short; z < -entry_z -> long
    # Exit: when z returns to a moderate level (abs(z) < exit_z)
    df['signal'] = 0
    long_condition = df['z'] < -entry_z
    short_condition = df['z'] > entry_z
    exit_condition = df['z'].abs() < exit_z
    
    # Use a simple state machine to avoid repeated entries
    position = 0
    for i in range(len(df)):
        if position == 0:
            if long_condition.iloc[i]:
                position = 1
                df.loc[df.index[i], 'signal'] = 1
            elif short_condition.iloc[i]:
                position = -1
                df.loc[df.index[i], 'signal'] = -1
        else:
            if exit_condition.iloc[i]:
                position = 0
                df.loc[df.index[i], 'signal'] = 0
            else:
                df.loc[df.index[i], 'signal'] = position  # hold
    
    return df['signal']
rationale: GBP/USD on weekly timeframe: large speculative positions (hedge funds) tend to overextend price beyond fair value, causing mean reversion when positioning unwinds; extreme z-score entry captures this reversal, exit when overextension fades.

self_critique.why_this_might_be_noise: Z-score thresholds are arbitrary and may not correspond to actual positioning extremes; mean reversion can be delayed or absent in trending regimes.
self_critique.what_would_disprove_this: If price continues trending after extreme z-score readings (no reversal), or if the strategy fails in out-of-sample periods with strong trends.
